Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The cursor is stuck at row [51,54], col [37,40] and can't move RIGHT or UP. ACTION7 doesn't do anything either. 
Looking at the image, the cursor is at the bottom-left corner of the bottom grid, but it's outside the grid. The cross is inside the grid at the bottom-left cell.
Maybe I need to reconsider the mechanic. In level 1, I clicked crosses directly and they moved. In level 2, clicking crosses doesn't work, and the cursor navigation doesn't seem to help either.
Wait - let me reconsider. Maybe the mechanic in level 2 is different. Perhaps I need to:
1. Navigate the cursor to a specific position
2. Then click a cross to move it to that position
Or maybe the cursor needs to be at a specific "target" position before clicking a cross.
Let me try a different approach: navigate the cursor back to the top grid area and see if clicking a cross there works when the cursor is nearby.
Actually, let me first check if the cursor can move into the top grid. Let me navigate back to the horizontal path and try to go UP into the top grid.
Wait, I already tried UP from the horizontal path and it didn't work. Let me try a different approach.
Maybe the mechanic is that I need to click the cross AND the cursor needs to be at a valid destination. Let me try navigating the cursor to an empty cell in the top grid and then clicking a cross.
But the cursor can't move UP from the horizontal path... Let me check if the cursor can move into the top grid from the right side.
Actually, looking at the image, the top grid has a path connecting to the right side. Let me try navigating the cursor to the right side of the top grid and see if it can enter.
Let me first move the cursor back to the horizontal path and then RIGHT to the right side, then UP to see if it can enter the top grid.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (updated):
- Cursor stuck at bottom, can't enter the grid
- ACTION7 doesn't do anything
- Need to reconsider the mechanic - maybe cursor needs to be at a destination before clicking a cross
Plan: Navigate cursor back to top grid area and try clicking crosses from there

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
from collections import Counter

# Navigate cursor back to the horizontal path
# Current: row=[51,54], col=[37,40]
# Need to go LEFT, then UP, then RIGHT to reach the top grid area

# First move LEFT to the junction
for i in range(5):
    result = action([{'action': 'LEFT'}])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    O_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O']
    for n in O_nodes:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"LEFT {i+1}: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Now move UP to the horizontal path
print("\n=== Move UP ===")
for i in range(5):
    result = action([{'action': 'UP'}])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    O_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O']
    for n in O_nodes:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"UP {i+1}: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Now move RIGHT to the right side
print("\n=== Move RIGHT ===")
for i in range(5):
    result = action([{'action': 'RIGHT'}])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    O_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O']
    for n in O_nodes:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"RIGHT {i+1}: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try UP to enter the top grid
print("\n=== Try UP to enter top grid ===")
result = action([{'action': 'UP'}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
O_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O']
for n in O_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"After UP: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
